turns.content: I got this error when trying to deserialize a large JSON payload into a String field: 'borrowed values may not be sent between a lock and release'. My colleague wrote code using serde_json::Deserializer with a String target, but it failed. What went wrong?

That's the classic borrow checker complaining about moving ownership across an interior mutability boundary or trying to clone where only borrowing is allowed. The issue usually stems from not understanding that deserializing into a mutable String consumes the input stream while simultaneously trying to keep a reference alive, which violates Rust's lifetime rules. You need to use serde::Deserialize with the 'de lifetime specifier and let your struct fields borrow from the input instead of owning them entirely. For example, you can declare your field as &'de str or Box<str> depending on whether you need zero-copy behavior or ownership transfer later.
Wait, so if I just change the type to use a borrowed reference like String::borrow() would that work? Or do I actually need to rewrite the struct definition?

You can't call String::borrow() directly because it doesn't exist in the standard library; you must define your field as a borrowed string slice or a smart pointer. The cleanest approach is to declare your field as &'de str within your struct, which tells serde_json that this field borrows from the deserialization input rather than consuming it. This avoids the clone operation entirely and prevents the borrow checker error by explicitly satisfying the lifetime constraints.
Okay, so my struct looks like struct MyData { name: &'de str }, right? And I also need to update serde attributes on that field?

Yes, exactly. You should add #[serde(borrow)] to your struct or the specific field containing the borrowed data. This attribute tells serde to treat those fields as borrows of the input stream rather than consuming them. When combined with the &'de type annotation, it allows you to deserialize large JSON payloads without allocating extra memory for temporary clones, achieving that zero-copy behavior you're looking for.
One more thing though, what happens if I try to use this borrowed field after deserialization is complete? Can I still mutate it?

You cannot mutate a &'de str directly because references are immutable by default. If you need mutability afterward, you should either clone the data into an owned String or Box<str> instead of keeping the lifetime bound. Alternatively, consider using a smart pointer like Arc<String> if multiple threads need to access the same borrowed value safely. The key distinction is that &'de str is read-only once deserialization finishes.
Got it, so for my use case where I only need to read the name field and pass it to another function, this should be fine without cloning?

Perfect, exactly. If your downstream logic only reads the value and never mutates it, then using &'de str with #[serde(borrow)] is ideal because it avoids unnecessary allocations. You can safely pass that borrowed string slice to any function that accepts &str or Box<str>, and you won't trigger the borrow checker warnings since you aren't trying to move ownership across boundaries.
What about when deserializing into a Vec<String>? Does this pattern apply similarly?

Yes, but it gets slightly more nuanced. For collections like Vec<String>, each element still needs explicit lifetime handling per field if you want true borrowing. You might define your struct with fields like name: &'de str and then iterate over the deserialized results to convert them into owned Strings only when needed. Alternatively, use serde::Deserialize for each vector item with borrow flags set individually so that large arrays can be processed without triggering intermediate allocations.
Is there a performance difference between using borrowed fields versus standard String fields in production code?

Yes significantly. Borrowed fields eliminate the clone overhead entirely during deserialization, which matters when processing millions of records or huge JSON files. You save both CPU cycles and memory bandwidth by avoiding temporary allocations for each field. However, you lose the ability to mutate those specific fields afterward without additional indirection like Arc or cloning, so choose based on whether your runtime needs mutation post-deserialization.
My team is debating whether to use this approach or stick with standard deserialization. Any pros and cons I should mention in our meeting?

The main pro is zero-copy deserialization which reduces memory pressure and latency for large payloads, plus better alignment with Rust's ownership semantics by avoiding unnecessary clones. The con is that you can't mutate those fields after loading unless you introduce indirection like Arc or Box<str>, so if your downstream logic requires modification, borrowed fields become less convenient. Also, debugging lifetime errors can be harder than standard String fields since the borrow checker complaints are more verbose and specific about exact locations.
I'm stuck on a production issue where my deserialized strings are going out of scope too early and causing panics. What's the difference between regular deserialization and using #[serde(borrow)]?

The core distinction is that standard deserialization owns the data, while #[serde(borrow)] borrows it. With normal serde, the parser takes ownership of the input string or slice, effectively moving it into your struct. When you use #[serde(borrow)], you tell the compiler to treat those values as references without taking ownership.
So if I don't use borrow, do I have to clone everything? That seems like a huge performance hit for large JSON payloads in our API.

You can avoid cloning entirely by using #[serde(borrow)] combined with the 'de lifetime. This creates zero-copy behavior where the data lives exactly as it did in the input buffer until explicitly consumed or dropped. You don't need to reallocate memory for every field.
But what about the lifetime annotation? How do I actually use 'de on my struct fields?

You declare it like this: struct MyData<'de> where each borrowed field is annotated with #[serde(borrow)] and you pass the lifetime explicitly in your deserialization call. The compiler ensures those references stay valid for exactly that duration.
Wait, so if I deserialize into a struct without 'de, why would my strings go out of scope?

Because the input buffer gets dropped when the deserialization function returns. If you borrowed from it but didn't extend its lifetime through your struct, those references become dangling pointers immediately after the call completes.
That sounds dangerous. Is there a way to keep the strings alive without manually managing lifetimes?

Not directly with just borrow, but you can wrap your struct in another type that owns the 'de lifetime and stores the borrowed data safely. Alternatively, use #[serde(borrow)] with a dedicated wrapper struct that lives long enough to hold those references.
Can you give me an analogy for why borrowing is different from copying?

Think of it like reading a book. Regular deserialization is photocopying every page into your own notebook—you create new paper and ink, which takes time and space. Borrowing is just pointing at the original pages without touching them; you only need the index finger, not extra materials.
So if I'm parsing JSON in a high-throughput service, avoiding those photocopies matters?

Absolutely. In a startup environment with millions of requests per second, avoiding allocations on every parse reduces latency and memory pressure significantly. The borrow approach lets you skip the allocation entirely.
One more thing though—what happens if I try to mutate those borrowed strings?
You can't. Borrowed references are immutable by design, so any attempt to modify them will cause a compile error or panic at runtime depending on how strict your checks are.
Got it. So the trade-off is safety and performance over mutability?
Exactly. You get zero-copy deserialization and better memory efficiency, but you sacrifice the ability to modify data in place without additional workarounds.

I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept for my Rust backend and I thought using `#[serde(borrow)]` would just let me deserialize big JSON payloads without copying the data, similar to how I used to read large files with `std::fs::File`. Is that right?

You have the general idea correct about avoiding copies, but there's a critical distinction in Rust memory safety. The `#[serde(borrow)]` attribute doesn't prevent allocation or copying of the data itself; instead, it changes how the deserialized values are stored relative to the input source. When you apply this macro to your struct fields, serde will borrow strings and slices from the input JSON rather than cloning them into owned heap memory. This means the lifetime of those borrowed references is tied directly to the original input document.
So if I deserialize a large configuration file using this attribute, the fields won't take up extra heap space? And what about the `serde_json::Value` type when it's converted into my struct?

Exactly regarding the heap space. By enabling borrowed deserialization with `#[serde(borrow)]`, your struct fields will hold references to the input buffer instead of owned strings or bytes vectors, which significantly reduces memory pressure for large payloads. Regarding `serde_json::Value`, when you convert a complex nested JSON structure into a Rust struct using this feature, every string and slice field becomes a reference with lifetime `'de` tied to the deserialization context. This allows you to process data without intermediate allocations, though you must ensure those references don't outlive the input document.

That sounds efficient but I'm worried about safety since my `impl Serialize` trait might try to write those borrowed fields back out. Can I still use normal serialization on these structs, or does it require a special version of the trait implementation?

You can absolutely use normal serialization on these structs without writing a custom `impl Serialize` for each borrowed field. The `serde` crate handles this automatically because when you serialize, it simply re-borrows the data from your struct back into the output buffer using the same lifetime rules. You don't need to manually implement any traits or manage lifetimes yourself; just ensure your struct definition uses the `#[serde(borrow)]` attribute consistently on all fields that should be borrowed.
I see, so I can use standard serialization logic. But what if my code tries to access one of these borrowed fields after the deserialization is done? Will it panic with a lifetime error?

Yes, that is exactly where you will encounter runtime safety constraints. If your code attempts to use a borrowed field from your struct after the input document has been dropped or if the reference scope ends prematurely, Rust's borrow checker will prevent it at compile time with clear lifetime errors involving `'de`. You cannot keep those references alive beyond the deserialization context unless you explicitly clone them first into owned types using `String` or `Vec<u8>`.
Got it, so I should probably just clone critical fields if I need to store them later. One last thing—does this work with generic collections like `serde_json::Map` or `serde_json::Array`?

Yes, borrowed deserialization works seamlessly with generic collections. When you define a struct field as `#[serde(borrow)] map: serde_json::Map<String, String>`, the entries in that map will also be references to the original input data rather than owned copies. This extends the zero-copy benefit throughout nested structures and arrays, making it ideal for deep nesting scenarios where traditional deserialization would cause exponential memory growth.
